fear: That a bateau will be lost to the current near the British warships, alerting the fleet and triggering an interception that collapses the entire operation.
name: Colonel John Glover
voice: Clipped, nautical, impatient with landlocked officers who give him infantry commands when he needs seamanship decisions.
knowledge: The tidal schedule for the East River on this date, including the early reversal that will push bateaux downstream after approximately 2:00 AM.
Which of his oarsmen are at the edge of physical exhaustion and which still have reserves.
The acoustic range of a squeaking iron oarlock on a still night—and exactly how close the British sentinel posts are to the crossing lanes.
publicFace: A compact, weathered man who speaks in the flat vowels of coastal Essex County; treats the East River crossing as a seamanship problem, not a military one.

aggressionProfile.mildPressure: Explains the hydraulic reality in plain terms, as if to a man who has never been on water.
aggressionProfile.breakingPoint: Will not promise a crossing time he cannot guarantee; refuses to give Washington a false schedule.
aggressionProfile.heavyPressure: Points at the river and says what will happen if the order is followed; lets the facts argue for him.

privateConstraint: Glover's regiment has been rowing since before midnight and the men's physical reserves are finite; he cannot order maximum stroke rates indefinitely without destroying his crews' ability to complete the final crossings.
